text: The patient is positioned in right lateral decubitus with slight pronation to facilitate lung folding. Two 3mm and two 5mm trocars are inserted, and a 5mm, 30-degree camera is advanced. CO₂ insufflation is used to collapse the lung, aided by a left bronchus blocker. The surgical field is inspected, identifying the phrenic, vagus, and recurrent laryngeal nerves. The crossing vein is dissected and divided using a Ligasure Maryland device.

The surgeon opens the mediastinal pleura from the left subclavian artery to the standing aorta, using cautery to seal the lymphatic vessels over the subclavian artery. The endopinat is utilized for a non-traumatic dissection.

The surgeon dissects the ligamentum arteriosum and secures it with vessel loops. Four hemolog clips are applied to ligate the ligament, ensuring complete occlusion. The clip applicator is used to automatically load and apply the clips, leaving two hemolog clips on each side of the cut ligament.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the left aortic arch, passing two vessel loops around it. A 30mm vascular endogia is advanced after removing a 5mm trocar, ensuring careful placement to avoid catching other structures and achieve complete occlusion of the aortic arch between the stapler marks. No staples are applied at the tip of the endogia.

Loose extra staples are removed using a suction device. Adhesive bands surrounding the esophagus and trachea are divided, allowing these structures to separate visibly. The surgeon checks for lymphatic leakage, places a chest tube, and leaves the mediastinal pleura widely open. Observable sutures are used to close the incision.
